review_summary: Transcript is sufficient for questions about fixed-window neural language models, what they improve over count tables, and why they still motivate RNNs. No separate clip is needed.

question_bank.question: What is the core architecture of the fixed-window neural language model?
choices: Concatenate embeddings from a local context, pass through a hidden layer, then predict the next word with softmax
Count all observed n-grams and renormalize them at inference time
Update a recurrent hidden state with shared weights across time
Sample random negative words and train only sigmoid classifiers
question_bank.explanation: The model forms a next-word distribution by taking a fixed context window of embeddings, combining them in a feedforward network, and applying softmax over the vocabulary.

transcript_quotes: concatenate embeddings from a fixed context window

question_bank.question: What problem did this early neural language model remove compared with plain count-based n-grams?
choices: The need to represent words as vectors
The need to store every observed n-gram explicitly
The need to predict the next word
The need to use any parameters
question_bank.explanation: Instead of storing all observed n-grams, the neural model stores learned parameters that generalize across contexts.

transcript_quotes: no longer have to store all observed n-grams

question_bank.question: Why does this model still fall short of the full language-modeling problem?
choices: It cannot output a probability distribution
It still throws away far-back context and handles each position with different weights
It requires teacher forcing to train
It can only be used for character-level text
question_bank.explanation: The model still makes a fixed-window Markov assumption and also treats the same word differently depending on which input slot it occupies.

transcript_quotes: same word differently depending on where it appears
